title: Perceived importance of science in treating anxiety or depression vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Share who say science helps a lot in treating anxiety or depression. Respondents were asked: "In general, how much do you think science helps us treat the following health problems? Does it help a lot, some, not much, or not at all?". GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
note: GDP per capita is expressed in [international-$](#dod:int_dollar_abbreviation) at 2021 prices.
caption:
Perceived importance of science in treating anxiety or depression vs. GDP per capita

Share who say science helps a lot in treating anxiety or depression. Respondents were asked: "In general, how much do you think science helps us treat the following health problems? Does it help a lot, some, not much, or not at all?". GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between the share of respondents who believe science helps a lot in treating anxiety or depression and the GDP per capita for various countries in 2020. The vertical axis represents the percentage of affirmative responses, ranging from 0% to over 50%, while the horizontal axis displays GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices on a logarithmic scale from roughly $1,000 to $100,000. Each circle represents a country, with its size proportional to the population. The data points are color-coded by region, including North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. Ethiopia and Senegal appear at the top left of the chart, indicating high perceived importance (near or above 50%) despite lower GDP per capita levels below $5,000. In contrast, countries like Hong Kong, Japan, and Bulgaria are positioned near the bottom right, showing lower shares of respondents (around 10% to 15%) despite higher GDP per capita figures exceeding $20,000. Large circles representing India and China are situated in the middle of the chart, with India showing a share near 30% at a GDP per capita around $6,000, and China showing a similar share at a GDP per capita near $20,000. The United States and Malta appear in the upper right quadrant, combining high GDP per capita above $50,000 with high perceived importance scores above 30%. European nations such as Spain, Belgium, and Ireland cluster in the mid-to-high range of both axes, while many African nations like Mali, Tanzania, and Burkina Faso group in the lower GDP range with varying levels of perceived importance between 20% and 50%.
